sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Renal and ureteric colic is caused by the passage of a calculus (stone) through the ureter. It is one of the most painful acute conditions encountered in a clinical setting.

**Incidence is significantly increased by dehydration and heat** — directly relevant in the mine site context. Young to middle-aged males are disproportionately affected.

### Must Differentiate From Aortic Emergencies
In any male >50 years with loin pain and vascular risk factors, **aortic dissection or ruptured AAA must be excluded before treating as renal colic**. An AAA can mimic renal colic and is fatal if missed.

### Stone Locations and Presentation
- Pelvi-ureteric junction (PUJ): flank pain, less colicky
- Mid-ureter: loin to groin radiation
- Vesico-ureteric junction (VUJ): urinary frequency, urgency, dysuria, referred scrotal/labial pain

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
### Classic Presentation
- **Sudden onset, severe, unilateral loin to groin pain**
- **Colicky** — comes in waves, peaks and partially resolves, then returns
- Patient is **unable to find a comfortable position** — writhes, cannot lie still (distinguishes from peritonism where movement worsens pain)
- Pain radiates from loin → iliac fossa → groin → inner thigh → scrotum/labia
- Nausea and vomiting (very common)
- Haematuria in ~80% (may be microscopic only)
- Ipsilateral renal angle (costovertebral) tenderness
- **No peritoneal signs** — no guarding, no rebound tenderness

### Red Flags — NOT Renal Colic: Consider AAA/Dissection
- Age >50 with vascular risk factors (hypertension, smoking, vascular disease)
- Pain that is **constant** rather than colicky
- Pain radiating to the **back** (interscapular or lower back)
- **Haemodynamic instability** — hypotension, tachycardia
- Pulsatile abdominal mass
- Syncope or near-syncope
- **If any doubt: treat as AAA — nil by mouth, IV access, urgent RFDS, cautious fluids**

### Complicated Colic — Requires Urgent Evacuation
- Fever with ureteric colic = infected obstructed kidney — surgical emergency
- Single kidney with obstruction
- Bilateral obstruction
- AKI (reduced urine output)
- Pain not controlled with analgesia

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
### NSAIDs Are First-Line Analgesia
- NSAIDs (ketorolac, ibuprofen) are more effective than opioids alone for renal colic
- Reduce ureteral spasm in addition to analgesia
- Use opioids if NSAIDs contraindicated (renal impairment, single kidney, allergy, haemodynamic instability)

### Hydration
- IV fluids are not proven to accelerate stone passage
- Encourage oral fluids if tolerating, but **do not force high oral intake** acutely — can worsen pain from increased renal pelvic pressure
- IV fluids for volume replacement if vomiting severely

### Urine Dipstick
- Blood on dipstick supports diagnosis (~80% sensitivity)
- Normal dipstick does NOT exclude renal colic
- Positive nitrites/leukocytes + loin pain + fever = infected obstruction → urgent surgery

### Stone Passage
- Stones <4 mm: ~80% pass spontaneously
- Stones 4–6 mm: ~50% pass
- Stones >6 mm: <20% pass spontaneously; usually require intervention

sections.title: Management - Primary
sections.content:
### Immediate Actions
1. **Exclude AAA** — assess for red flags above before starting analgesics
2. **Analgesia** — aggressive, don't undertreated
3. **Urine dipstick** — check for blood, leucocytes, nitrites
4. **IV access** — all patients (vomiting may require IV fluids; opioids IV if NSAIDs insufficient)
5. **Temperature** — fever + loin pain = surgical emergency, call RFDS immediately

### Analgesia Protocol
**Step 1 — NSAID (first-line):**
- Ketorolac 30 mg IM or 15–30 mg IV (avoid if renal impairment, single kidney, haemodynamic instability)
- OR Ibuprofen 400 mg PO if able to tolerate orally

**Step 2 — Antispasmodic:**
- Hyoscine butylbromide (Buscopan) 20 mg IV or IM — smooth muscle relaxation

**Step 3 — Opioid if NSAID insufficient:**
- Morphine 2.5–5 mg IV titrated, or fentanyl 25–50 mcg IV

**Step 4 — Antiemetic:**
- Ondansetron 4–8 mg IV/oral, or metoclopramide 10 mg IV

### Fever + Loin Pain (Infected Obstruction)
- This is a **surgical emergency** — infected obstructed kidney can cause fatal sepsis within hours
- IV access, IV antibiotics (ceftriaxone 1 g IV)
- IV fluids
- Urgent RFDS — requires emergency urological drainage

### Evacuation Indications
- Fever (any temperature with loin pain)
- Pain not controlled with above protocol
- Suspected AAA
- Single kidney or bilateral stones
- AKI or anuric
- First presentation (requires CT urogram for diagnosis and stone characterisation)

sections.title: Management - Advanced
sections.content:
### Septic Shock from Infected Obstruction
If hypotension + fever + loin pain:
- Treat per sepsis-septic-shock guideline
- IV ceftriaxone 2 g stat
- Aggressive fluid resuscitation
- Vasopressors if not responding to fluids
- Urgent RFDS — this patient requires emergency intervention

### Failed Analgesia
- If pain not controlled with NSAID + opioid combination:
- Consider ketamine 0.1–0.3 mg/kg IV titrated (sub-dissociative dose) for severe refractory pain
- Reassess for complications (expanding AAA, infected obstruction)

### Pain Resolved — Observation Period
- Resolution of pain does not indicate stone has passed
- Stone may have moved to less obstructed position temporarily
- Patient still requires evacuation for imaging and urological assessment if first episode or complications present

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
### Prevention — Mine Site Context
- Adequate daily fluid intake (2.5–3 L/day minimum in tropical/hot conditions)
- Electrolyte replacement drinks to maintain mineral balance
- Workers who have had one stone have ~50% chance of recurrence within 5 years

### Dietary Advice (On Discharge)
- Increase fluid intake
- Reduce salt and animal protein intake
- For calcium oxalate stones (most common): moderate calcium intake, reduce oxalate-rich foods (spinach, nuts, chocolate)

### Return to Work
- Workers with untreated stones may pass them at any time
- Risk of sudden incapacitation — consider fitness-for-work implications for safety-critical roles
- Advise to notify medical staff if symptoms recur
